coredata.dc:title: Gold nanoparticles/glycine derivatives/graphene quantum dots composite with tunable fluorescence and surface enhanced Raman scattering signals for cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jiefei
Qin, Lixia
Kang, Shi-Zhao
Li, Guodong
Li, Xiangqing
coredata.dc:description: Controllable assembly of the hybrids composed of various types of nanoscale objects provides new opportunities for material fabrication. Herein, the tunable bi-functionalities of fluorescence and surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) signals were collected by the assembled composites of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs)/glycine derivatives (Gn, n =0, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 9)/graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The composition and structure of the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composites were characterized by Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). Results indicated that the fluorescence and SERS response could be tuned effectively by changing the chain length of Gn. Also, the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composites could be used in a wide pH range and exhibited good stability at room and physiological temperatures. Moreover, the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composites displayed low cytotoxicity in A549 cells, and the cell viability was found to be above 90% at concentrations ranging from 6.42 to 200μg/mL, which made AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composites very attractive for biological imaging applications in vivo.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticles
Graphene quantum dots
Bridged composite
Bi-functionality
Biological imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SERS response of the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composite
The influence of pH value on the fluorescence of the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composite
Fluorescence images of the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composite in cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectra of G6 (a), AuNPs/G6 (b) and AuNPs/G6/GQDs (c); (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNPs (a), AuNPs/G6 (b) and AuNPs/G6/GQDs (c).
TEM images of AuNPs (a), AuNPs/G6 (b), and high-resolution TEM images of AuNPs/G6 (c) and AuNPs/G6/GQDs (d). The inset in a: size distribution histogram for AuNPs.

Fluorescence spectra of AuNPs/GQDs and AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composite. (a) AuNPs/GQDs and AuNPs/G0/GQDs; (b) AuNPs/GQDs and AuNPs/G3/GQDs; (c) AuNPs/GQDs and AuNPs/G4/GQDs; (d) AuNPs/GQDs and AuNPs/G5/GQDs; (e) AuNPs/GQDs and AuNPs/G6/GQDs; (f) AuNPs/GQDs and AuNPs/G9/GQDs, λex =320nm. (g) Plot of ∆F versus Gn(n =0, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 9). Error bars depict the standard deviations. Standard deviations are calculated from three replicate samples (n =3).

(A) Raman spectra of the CV aqueous solution (0.1M) on glass substrate (a) and the CV aqueous solutions (2.5×10−5 M) on various substrates. Glass substrate (b), AuNPs (c), GQDs (d), AuNPs/GQDs (e), AuNPs/G0/GQDs (f), AuNPs/G3/GQDs (g), AuNPs/G4/GQDs (h), AuNPs/G5/GQDs (i), AuNPs/G6/GQDs (j) and AuNPs/G9/GQDs (k). (B) Column chart of SERS intensities of the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs (n =0, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 9) based on the bands at 1170cm−1, 1384cm−1 and 1614cm−1.

Fluorescence microscopic images of A549 cells incubated with the AuNPs/G6/GQDs composite. a) Bright-field image of the AuNPs/G6/GQDs composite loaded with A549 cells; b) Fluorescence image of the AuNPs/G6/GQDs composite loaded with A549 cells; (c) Merged images of the AuNPs/G6/GQDs composite loaded with A549 cells.
Illustration of the preparation of AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composite and the structure of Gn.

head.ce:title: Gold nanoparticles/glycine derivatives/graphene quantum dots composite with tunable fluorescence and surface enhanced Raman scattering signals for cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Center of Graphene Research, Shanghai Institute of Technology, 100 Haiquan Road, Shanghai 201418, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The biofunctional-composites of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs)/glycine derivatives (Gn, n =0, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 9)/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were assembled. The fluorescence and SERS response could be tuned effectively by changing the chain length of Gn. Also, the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composites displayed low cytotoxicity in living cells, which made AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composites very attractive for biological imaging applications in vivo.

Controllable assembly of the hybrids composed of various types of nanoscale objects provides new opportunities for material fabrication. Herein, the tunable bi-functionalities of fluorescence and surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) signals were collected by the assembled composites of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs)/glycine derivatives (Gn, n =0, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 9)/graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The composition and structure of the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composites were characterized by Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). Results indicated that the fluorescence and SERS response could be tuned effectively by changing the chain length of Gn. Also, the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composites could be used in a wide pH range and exhibited good stability at room and physiological temperatures. Moreover, the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composites displayed low cytotoxicity in A549 cells, and the cell viability was found to be above 90% at concentrations ranging from 6.42 to 200μg/mL, which made AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composites very attractive for biological imaging applications in vivo.

ce:section.ce:para: The combination of different nanomaterials will be beneficial to the development of multi-functional nano-platforms. Recently, many reports have focused on constructing the multi-functional nano-materials that are from various nanoparticles including gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) [1,2], silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) [3,4], and quantum dots (QDs) [5]. Researches show that these nanoparticles primarily encapsulated with various types of polymers and inorganic shells endow the nano-platforms with unique optical properties for high-throughput detection and multimodal imaging.

With the purpose of introducing multi-functionalities into the hybrids, AuNPs were primarily chosen because of their facile assemblies, fascinating electronic and optical properties, thus, AuNPs have been widely used in traditional functional nanocomposites [6–8], such as advanced electronic sensors [9], biosensors [10], and electrodes for catalytic systems [11]. Also, AuNPs become one of the most representative metal nanoparticles, and many surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) substrates based on Au have fabricated, such as Au nanocages [12], and holey Au-Ag alloy nanoplates [13]. Until now, the Au based SERS technique has been applied to many fields, such as sensing and trace analysis [14,15]. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are increasingly drawing the people's attention due to their stable photoluminescence, good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and chemical inertia [16,17]. Moreover, their strong quantum confinement and edge effects are advantageous for electronic and optoelectronic applications in bioimaging [18], metal sensing [19], photovoltaic [20] and photocatalysts [21]. Based on above excellent properties of AuNPs and GQDs, the combination of AuNPs and GQDs would develop the multi-functionalities of hybrids. Thus, the SERS and fluorescence signals could be collected by the constructed AuNPs/GQDs system.

Because of the strong distance dependence of metal/semiconductor hybrids, controlling the spatial positioning of metal and semiconductor components using a variety of spacer materials (such as DNA and polypeptides) is a critical challenge [22,23]. It is well known that amino acids and peptides are effective building blocks for creating hierarchical nanomaterials [24] and functionalized AuNPs [25]. A multitude of functionalities and unique secondary structures based on controllable primary sequences and environmental conditions were constructed [26]. By using amino acids and peptides, it is thus possible to tailor new hybrid materials for broadened applications in medicine, bionanotechnology, and electrochemistry [27]. Also, incorporation of different sequences of amino acids into nano-object design provides a powerful platform for the assembly of nanoscale objects in a highly controllable fashion. Glycine (G0) is a relatively simple amino acid. Moreover, its synthesis and bio-conjunction is easier to be further designed. Thus, a series of derivatives of Gn could be synthesized (Gn, n =3, 4, 5, 6 and 9) and be used to tune the fluorescence and SERS response of the AuNPs/GQDs system.

In this paper, with Gn as a bridge, AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composites with tunable fluorescence and SERS signals for cellular imaging are fabricated. The fluorescence and SERS response of the composites could be tuned effectively by changing the chain length of Gn (which adjusts the distance between AuNPs and GQDs). In addition, the influence of pH value on fluorescence of AuNPs/G6/GQDs, its time-dependent fluorescence behavior and biological imaging applications in vivo are also demonstrated.

Chloroauric acid (99%, HAuCl4) was purchased from Shanxi Kaida Chemical Engineering Company. Glycine (G0) and its derivatives (99%, Gn, n =3, 4, 5, 6 and 9) were purchased from Shanghai Bioscience & Technology Company. Sodium citrate (99%, C6H5Na3O7·H2O), sulfuric acid (98%, H2SO4), hydrochloric acid (99%, HCl), sodium hydroxide (99%, NaOH), hydrogen peroxide (30%, H2O2) and graphite powder (>99.85%) with an average size of 30μm and other chemicals were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Company. All the reagents were used without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

AuNPs were synthesized based on a reported literature [28], Au colloids with an average diameter of 50±5nm were prepared by dropwise addition of 30mL of an aqueous solution of sodium citrate (0.01g/mL) to a boiling aqueous solution of HAuCl4 (3.1×10−4 g/mL) under vigorous stirring. The reaction mixture was boiled for 1h, and then cooled to room temperature naturally. The resulting particles were well suspended in H2O [29]. The Au colloid was centrifuged for three times (5000rpm), and the solid obtained was dispersed in distilled water (100mL).

GO was prepared by a modified Hummers method [30]. GQDs were prepared by using GO as the main reactant [31]. In brief, 250mg of GO was dissolved in 15mL of DMF and sonicated for 30min. Then, the GO/DMF mixture was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and kept at 200°C for 5h. Then, the reactors were cooled down to room temperature. The product contained brown transparent suspension and black precipitate. The black precipitate was removed by suction filtration, and the filtrate was purified by column chromatography on silica gel, using water as the eluent. The eluent was concentrated by the rotary evaporator, and then the GQDs aqueous solution was achieved.

The preparation procedure for the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composite is described in Scheme 1 . In brief, 3mL of 4.79×10−5 M Gn (n =0, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 9) solution was added into 100μL of AuNPs aqueous solution, respectively, and incubated for 30min, and then washed for three times with deionized water to remove excess Gn. After that, 100μL of GQDs (0.1mg/mL) was added into the AuNPs/Gn aqueous solution and the reaction was incubated for 5min at room temperature. The excess GQDs were removed by centrifugation and subsequently washed with deionized water for three times. In addition, the AuNPs/GQDs was prepared by direct addition of GQDs into AuNPs aqueous solution.

The Raman spectra of all samples were measured by thei-Raman® (USA) with integration time of 2200ms, multiplier of 2, and time average of 2. An excitation wavelength of 785nm was used to induce Raman scattering. The SERS substrates were prepared by coating AuNPs, AuNPs/GQDs or AuNPs/Gn/GQDs dispersion on glass substrates, respectively. Then, a certain amount of crystal violet (CV) aqueous solution (2.5×10−5 M) was dropped onto the treated substrate, after drying and forming a uniform layer. The Raman spectra of the substrates were collected at different spots. For comparison, the Raman spectrum of the glass substrate only dropped with 2.5×10−5 M CV solution was also measured.

A549 cells were cultured in a flask in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium supplemented with heat-inactivated bovine serum (10%), 100U/mL penicillin and 100U/mL streptomycin, and were maintained at 37°C in a humidified atmosphere (5% CO2 and 95% air). The cells were seeded in 1.5cm dishes and allowed to adhere for 10h before treatment. Then, cells were incubated with fresh media containing 200μg/mL AuNPs/G6/GQDs. After 10h, the growth medium was removed, and the cells were washed for several times with PBS. The cover glass was then mounted on the microscopic glass slide with the cells and was studied under a microscope.

The cytotoxicity assay was performed by the MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) method. A549 cells were placed in 96-well culture plates (104 cells/well), and allowed to attach for 12h before treatment. The cells were treated with AuNPs/G6/GQDs ranging from 6.42 to 200μg/mL. The cell viability was evaluated by the MTT assay after 24h treatment. The optical density (OD) in control and samples-treated wells was measured in an automated microplate reader (Multiskan Ex, Lab systems, Finland) at a test wavelength of 320nm. The cytotoxicity of AuNPs/G6/GQDs was expressed as IC50 (concentration of 50% cytotoxicity, which was extrapolated from linear regression analysis of the experimental data).

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-3900 spectrophotometer (Japan). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a HITACHI F-4600 spectrometer (Japan) at room temperature with an excitation wavelength at 320nm. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopic analysis was carried out with Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (USA) in the scanning range of 500–4000cm−1 at room temperature. A KBr pellet was utilized for the purpose of calibration. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images and the high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were taken with a JEOL JEM-2100F microscope device (Japan), and the accelerating voltage was 200kV. The TEM samples were prepared by placing drops of the sample dispersion on a carbon-coated copper grid and dried at room temperature. All fluorescence images of cellular experiments were acquired by Shimadzu Cary Eclipse (Varian) fluorometer with a wide-field inverted fluorescence microscopy (Nikon-Ti, Co. Ltd. Japan) using a 60×1.2 NA objective and a matched electron multiplying charge-coupled device (EMCCD) (Roper).

Scheme 1 shows the structure of Gn and the schematic formation of AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composite. Firstly, AuNPs were modified by Gn due to the strong interaction between the terminal amino groups of Gn and AuNPs, and the AuNPs/Gn was obtained. According to the structure of Gn, the edge of AuNPs/Gn has abundant carboxyl groups which are easy to be linked with GQDs by the dehydration between hydroxyl groups of GQDs and some carboxylic groups of Gn. Subsequently, the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composites were achieved.

The FT-IR spectra of G6, AuNPs/G6 and AuNPs/G6/GQDs are presented in Fig. 1 A. As shown in FT-IR spectrum of G6 (Fig. 1A(a)), the characteristic bands of OH (3432cm−1), CO (3088cm−1, 1284cm−1 and 1249cm−1), NH2 (3280cm−1, 1559cm−1 and 1415cm−1) were observed obviously. However, in the FT-IR spectrum of AuNPs/G6 (Fig. 1A(b)), the bands at 3280cm−1, 1559cm−1 and 1415cm−1 assigned to the stretching vibration of NH2 of G6 disappear, which indicates that G6 molecules were anchored on AuNPs. Also, after GQDs were introduced into the AuNPs/G6, the strong band at 3432cm−1 assigned to the stretching vibration of OH (Fig. 1A(a)) is blue-shifted to 3448cm−1. It is suggested that the GQDs could be bonded on the surface of the AuNPs/G6 by the dehydration condensation reaction between hydroxyl groups of GQDs and the carboxylic groups of the G6 in the AuNPs/G6.

The typical UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNPs, AuNPs/G6 and AuNPs/G6/GQDs are studied (Fig. 1B). Clearly, a strong absorption at 539nm is observed, which corresponds to surface plasmon resonance in AuNPs (Fig. 1B(a)). In the absorption spectrum of the AuNPs/G6 (Fig. 1B(b)), the band at 539nm for AuNPs is red-shifted to 541nm, which is due to the increase of local refractive index around the particles after the formation of the G6 capped AuNPs [32]. After GQDs interacted with the G6 in the AuNPs/G6, the spectrum of the obtained sample (AuNPs/G6/GQDs) is slightly red-shifted to 542nm (Fig. 1B(c)). Furthermore, the surface plasmon resonance peaks of the AuNPs in various AuNPs/Gn/GQDs (Gn, n =0, 3, 4, 5 and 9) are discussed (Table S1, ESI†). Clearly, the surface plasmon resonance peaks are red-shifted compared to that of single AuNPs. It should be noted that the red-shift is slightly increased when the Gn in the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs is changed from G0, G3, G4, G5, G6 to G9. The above results further confirm that the Gn can interact with AuNPs and GQDs, respectively, and the interaction between AuNPs and GQDs could be adjusted by changing the chain length of Gn.

Moreover, the morphologies of the AuNPs, AuNPs/G6 and AuNPs/G6/GQDs composites are observed by TEM (Fig. 2 ). As shown in Fig. 2a, clearly, the AuNPs are monodispersed with spherical shape. The size of AuNPs ranges from 45nm to 60nm, and it is focused on 50nm, as illustrated in the inset of Fig. 2a. Fig. 2b and c show the HRTEM images of AuNPs/G6. Clearly, AuNPs are coated with uniform transparent layers, which indicate that the Gn has been coated on the surface of AuNPs. As we can see from the HRTEM image of the AuNPs/G6/GQDs (Fig. 2d), more GQDs (the size is focused on 5nm) inserted into the AuNPs/G6 or adhered to the surface of the AuNPs/G6 could be observed clearly, and the size of GQDs is in agreement with the bare GQDs (Fig. S1, ESI†). In Fig. 2d, the G6 layer is not seen obviously in that it could be destroyed when the electron beam with high intensity bombarded it. By means of the results of FT-IR, UV–vis and TEM, we can conclude that the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composites are successfully fabricated.

What influence do the distance between AuNPs and GQDs have on the fluorescence of the composite? In order to obtain the information, Gn (n =0, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 9) was introduced as the linker of AuNPs and GQDs, and obtained a serials of AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composites with different spacing distance. As shown in Fig. 3a–f, interestingly, when the Gn in the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs is changed from G0, G3, G4, G5, G6 to G9, the fluorescence peak (λem =423nm) intensity of the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs increases gradually, and the increment ∆ F (F AuNPs/Gn/GQDs-F AuNPs/GQDs) is about 0, 49, 184, 376, 532 and 589, respectively. The enhanced fluorescence peak (λem =423nm) intensity (∆ F) of AuNPs/Gn/GQDs was calculated according to the equation ∆ F = F-F
0 (F and F
0 represent the fluorescence intensity of AuNPs/Gn/GQDs and AuNPs/GQDs, respectively) [33]. The plot of ∆ F versus Gn (n =0, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 9) is illustrated in Fig. 3g. It is well known that the fluorescence of quantum dots could be quenched by metal particles to some extent [34]. It is reported that increasing distance between AuNPs and quantum dots can decrease the fluorescence quenching of AuNPs to quantum dots, and result in the obvious fluorescence recovery of quantum dots [35]. It should be noted that the increment ∆F for the AuNPs/G0/GQDs is 0 because G0 is with a shorter chain. The increment ∆ F for the AuNPs/G6/GQDs is close to the AuNPs/G9/GQDs, which may be due to the poor solubility and good flexibility of G9 with long chain. In short, the Gn (n =3, 4, 5, 6 and 9) is the appropriate option to adjust the spacing distance between AuNPs and the GQDs, which make the fluorescence response of the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composite could be tuned effectively to a large extent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS response of the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composite
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, with crystal violet (CV) as the probe molecule, we explored the potential of the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs for SERS applications. Raman spectra of the 0.1M CV and 2.5×10−5 M CV aqueous solution on the glass substrate (Fig. 4a, Fig. 4b), AuNPs (Fig. 4c), GQDs (Fig. 4d), AuNPs/GQDs (Fig. 4e), AuNPs/G0/GQDs (Fig. 4f), AuNPs/G3/GQDs (Fig. 4g), AuNPs/G4/GQDs (Fig. 4h), AuNPs/G5/GQDs (Fig. 4i), AuNPs/G6/GQDs (Fig. 4j) and AuNPs/G9/GQDs (Fig. 4k) are shown in Fig. 4A. When the concentration of CV is high (0.1M), the SERS peaks of CV on glass substrate can be observed clearly. However, when the concentration of CV is 2.5×10−5 M, no Raman signals for CV are observed on the glass substrate. It is indicated that the glass substrate is not sensitive to CV with low concentration. As shown in Fig. 4c, the SERS peaks of the CV (2.5×10−5 M) on only AuNPs are also very weak. In addition, as shown in Fig. 4d, no characteristic Raman peaks of the CV (2.5×10−5 M) on GQDs (0.1mg/mL) are observed, either. It is indicated that only GQDs is not a sensitive substrate for CV with low concentration (2.5×10−5 M). Moreover, Raman peaks of GQDs itself are not observed. The reason is in that the excitation wavelength determines the Raman peaks of GQDs itself [36]. Therefore, all change of Raman signals in Fig. 4d–k can be attributed to CV. Surprisingly, the characteristic Raman bands of CV (2.5×10−5 M) at 416cm−1, 722cm−1, 1170cm−1, 1384cm−1 and 1614cm−1 are observed on AuNPs/GQDs (e) and AuNPs/G0/GQDs (f), which are in good agreement with the Raman spectra of 0.1M CV on glass substrate (a). It was reported that GQDs have great potential for efficient SERS applications [36]. Therefore, the intense Raman response is ascribed to the synergistic effect of AuNPs and GQDs [37]. In addition, G0 has little influence on SERS response of the AuNPs/GQDs due to the short chain of G0. When the Gn in the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs is changed from G3, G4 to G5 (g–i), the intensity of characteristic Raman bands of CV decreases, indicating that the synergistic effect is decreased followed with the increased spacing distance of AuNPs and GQDs. Interestingly, when Gn is changed from G6 to G9 (j–k), the intensity of characteristic Raman bands of CV increases gradually. This phenomenon demonstrated that the synergistic effect is not only dependent on the distance but also the flexibility of amino acids [38]. The possible explanation is that the increased chain length would make the Gn chain bend down to the surface of the AuNPs, and the distance of AuNPs and GQDs would be shortened, thus, the synergistic effect reoccurred. The column chart of SERS intensities of the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs (n =0, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 9) based on the bands at 1170cm−1, 1384cm−1 and 1614cm−1 as representative is illustrated in Fig. 4B. It clearly shows that the SERS intensity of the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs could be tuned effectively by Gn (n =0, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 9). Based on above results, it is concluded that Gn (n =3, 4, 5, 6 and 9) could not only effectively tune the fluorescence intensity of AuNPs/Gn/GQDs but also its SERS intensity by adjusting the spacing distance between AuNPs and GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of pH value on the fluorescence of the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composite
ce:section.ce:para: Influence of chemical environment on fluorescence of the AuNPs/G6/GQDs as a representative was investigated by adjusting pH values of the solution (Fig. S2, ESI†). The plot of fluorescence intensity (λem =423nm) versus various pH values is illustrated in Fig. S2B. Clearly, the fluorescence of the composite is stable in the pH range of 4.5 to 10.8. It could be attributed to the fluorescence species in the GQDs, which have basic and acidic sites relevant to the photoluminescence emission [39]. Therefore, the composite could be used in a wide pH range. When the pH value is <4.5, the fluorescence intensity of the AuNPs/G6/GQDs decreases gradually, and the emission peak is red-shifted, maybe due to the aggregation of composite. Also, the fluorescence intensity decreases slightly when the pH value is higher than 10.8. Thus, under strong acidic or alkaline condition, the fluorescence intensity of the AuNPs/G6/GQDs is lower. It is indicated that the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs system could be used in the range of weak acidic to weak alkaline condition. It provides a potential application for the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composite in broad biological imaging.

In order to simulate the stability of AuNPs/G6/GQDs in vitro, the time-dependent fluorescence behaviors of the AuNPs/G6/GQDs are investigated at room temperature (25°C) and physiological temperature (37°C). Figs. 3B and S3A show the fluorescence intensity of the AuNPs/G6/GQDs composite at 25°C and 37°C in the range of 0–120h, respectively. Surprisingly, fluorescence intensities of the AuNPs/G6/GQDs at 25°C and 37°C during the 120h are hardly changed, and the shape of the emission peak is also hardly changed. Therefore, the prepared AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composite possesses stable fluorescence for a long time, which indicates that the fabricated composite could be used in vivo for biological detection and imaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence images of the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composite in cells
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above results, the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composite is used in the fluorescence imaging of A549 cells. The AuNPs/G6/GQDs with strong fluorescence is selected as a representative to investigate the imaging in cells and the cytotoxicity to cells. Fig. 5 shows the fluorescence images of the AuNPs/G6/GQDs incubated with A549 cells for 10h. Clearly, the AuNPs/G6/GQDs loaded cells displays a bright fluorescence in both the cytoplasm and the nucleus of A549 cells (Fig. 5b and 5c), indicating that the composite is efficiently internalized by the cells, even without help of any other transfection technique. Also, bright-field measurement (Fig. 5a) clearly shows that the cells are viable throughout the imaging experiments. These images suggest the potential of the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composites as robust biomarkers due to their strong fluorescence. Moreover, we assessed the cytotoxicity of AuNPs/G6/GQDs at different concentrations using the MTT assay (Fig. S4, Supporting Information). After addition of AuNPs/G6/GQD, the cell viability was found to be above 90% at concentrations ranging from 6.42 to 200μg/mL. Results indicated that the AuNPs/G6/GQDs probe with low cytotoxicity will be suitable for use in a variety of biological applications.

In conclusion, with Gn as the linker between AuNPs and GQDs, the AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composites were successfully fabricated by a simple method. It was found that the interaction between AuNPs and GQDs can be adjusted by changing the chain length of Gn. Furthermore, the tunable bi-functionalities of SERS signals and fluorescence were collected by the assembled AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composite, and the SERS and fluorescence intensity of AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composites can be effectively tuned by changing the spacing distance between AuNPs and GQDs. Also, the composite (AuNPs/G6/GQDs) exhibited good stability and could be used in a broad pH range. Importantly, the AuNPs/G6/GQDs composite displayed low cytotoxicity and strong fluorescence in cells. Thus, it is selected as a representative for further applications, such as, the detection of the biological small molecules will be our future work using the bi-functionalities of AuNPs/Gn/GQDs composite.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Innovation Program of Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (No. 15ZZ096), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21301118, 21305092 and 21371070).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2017.02.083.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Au@Organosilica multifunctional nanoparticles for the multimodal imaging
